speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape number 3 with Suse Rosenstock, January 19th, 1996, in San Diego. OK, you were just starting to talk about the bombings and the gas masks.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well the bombings, of course, were always with us. The gas masks were always with us. They're horrible things. You can't breathe in them. In the middle of-- in June, 1944, there was hope that somebody in the family was surviving. I got a telegram from a great aunt in Switzerland. And the telegram said, having good news from your mother. Are well. And if possible, inform Aunt Alice. Aunt Alice was my mother's only surviving sister in New York. When that telegram came, I said to my foster parents, I have a mother and a sister, but my father is dead. And I guess this is where ESP comes in, or whatever you want to call it. There was no way that my foster parents could convince me that that word 'are doing well' meant that they're all well. I just singled out that it was my mother and my sister that were alive, and my father was dead. And of course, it turned out, indeed, that it was that way.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did happen to your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father died in Terezin in 1940-- October of 1942. He-- they had been taken there-- my mother, my sister, and my father had been taken there in July of 1942. And he died in, as I say, in October. I thank God that he didn't have to suffer anymore. He had already suffered once in Buchenwald, and God was good to him. But of course, as I said, that telegram, you know, that was something that I hung onto. And in November, 1944, I was taken from my foster family and put into a Jewish hostel in Birmingham, where there were 27 other Kindertransport kinder. We lived together. We had a house mother and father. I did finish school, because you have to go to school till you're 14 in England. I did not sit for the secondary school exam because that costs money, and there wasn't any. And then I went to work for the social service department of Singers Hill Synagogue in Birmingham. It was a terrible experience. Again, the woman who was my boss was a mean person. The hostel again, it was-- I was on my own. The hostel took some of the money that I made, and I kept very little. And I was supposed to take care of myself, but it was barely enough. And that's where, of course, my Judaism came back. I attended synagogue. And, of course, in the hostel we celebrated the holidays as a, as a group. And of course, we all came from varied backgrounds-- some religious, some secular, some very, very assimilated. And it was-- belonged to various Jewish groups, such as Habonim or Hashomer Hatzair. And there was always a conflict of what you did. But it was a good camaraderie that we had. And it has remained. Many of us who were in that hostel have remained friends. Many of them-- many of the young people lost their families. And so of course, we needed one another. And by that time lists were coming out of Europe, of survivors and of those who did not survive. And so every day when we came home from work, we would pore over these lists-- who found somebody, and who didn't. We mourned with those who lost, and we rejoiced with those who found. And one day it was my turn to rejoice. A telegram found me from Poland that my mother and my sister, indeed, had survived Hell. My sister had remembered my address in her head through all those horrible years, and had given it to a Russian soldier, who was among the, sort of, liberators, who wanted to help them. And of course, her first priority was to let me know that they were alive.

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't have money to answer them. And I went to the office of the hostel, to the secretary. And she refused me money. Which, of course, needless to say upset me terribly. But my foster father and mother followed up, and telephoned me, and said, did you answer the telegram? And I lied, yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: But my foster father saw through it. And they came running to Birmingham on that Saturday afternoon, and took me to the main post office in Birmingham to answer the telegram. Of course, it didn't matter one way or the other because by that time my sister and my mother had left the town. So that-- it was already a moot question. But it was just important enough for them to say, you've got to answer it. And so, anxiously, I waited. And the next communication came from the town of Duisburg, where my mother and my sister had returned and gone to a convalescent home and then into an apartment. And the correspondence came through the British Army, because there was no surface mail to civilians yet at that time. And my mother, of course, told me of my father's death. And then many months later came a letter that's also precious to me at this point, is the one letter-- the first letter that they wrote on a piece of brown paper, in pencil, from Berlin, that they had found their way back to Berlin and just to tell me that they were alive. But that letter was received way after the one through the army. And of course, we corresponded. One of the soldiers that my mother had befriended came to England on leave and brought me things-- cookies that my mother had baked me and all sorts of little things that mother had bought for me, trinkets. And he also left me a ticket to come, to go to London to a friend for Pesach that year, in 1945-- '46. '46. And I was still waiting for a visa to come to America. Well, I went to London for Pesach. The next morning I had my call to go to the embassy. And they finally granted me a visa for America. I came to America, and on May 13th, I landed here, 1946. And on February the 11th in 1947, I was finally reunited with my mother and my sister in New York, on the pier.

speaker: interviewer
text: After what, eight years?

speaker: interviewee
text: After eight years. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that moment like?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was an incredible moment. I jumped over a barrier on the pier-- that I have no idea how I did-- and I ran down the pier to greet them. It was a difficult time to re-adjust. Wasn't easy. My mother and my sister had a special relationship. I was not part of it. And we all worked very hard to establish a family relationship once again. I realized that we all had to work together to do it. And I put-- I have to tell you, I put every effort that I possibly could into it. And it certainly paid off. My sister and I are extremely close. My mother lived to see my grandson-- her great grandson-- before she died. She saved-- she did what she could to save her family. And she deserves an awful lot of credit for that. It's not easy to send away a child. I've looked at each of my children as they reach the age of eight. I have three. Elaine is now almost 44. Alan is 41. And Debbie is 38. I looked at each one of them and wondered if I could have that strength to do what she did.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you understand it, as a kid?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think so.

speaker: interviewer
text: That it wasn't rejection?

speaker: interviewee
text: I did not-- no. I did not view it as a rejection. I know it has been viewed as that by others. No. No, I've argued the fact that it was saving the family. And here again, I have-- you know, it's, it's, it's producing more Jews, procreating, saving the family name. It's all part of it. You know, at least one-- and as her comment was, at least one member of the family can stay alive. Had I been there, we would not have survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you feel when Edith and your mother told you what exactly they had gone through?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have a lot of guilt about that. I have a lot of guilt about living in the lap of luxury, basically, and they having gone through hell and back. I want to do that odyssey that they did. I know I won't see much, but maybe it will put something to rest in me. I don't know. I still intend to do it someday. It hurts. It hurts to see, you know, my sister today. Of course, she's happy, and she has a wonderful family and a wonderful outlook. And that is something that we have both, I think, gotten from our mother, is this thing that you remember the past, and live the present, and look to the future. I hope I've taught that to my children. And I hope that this story will help them understand. And help others understand. We were taken away from families. We didn't have families, we had strangers to bring us up with different-- although good-- values, but different values than would have happened.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I often think, what would I be had I been-- had I remained with my family. Would I be different? I don't think much different, really, because as I said, I was given good values. I was given the proper values to grow up with. And that I'm very grateful for. Do you ever imagine what your fate might have been if you had stayed with them in Europe?

speaker: interviewee
text: In England?

speaker: interviewer
text: I mean in Germany.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh in Germany. I don't know. It's hard to--

speaker: interviewer
text: Because you said you felt--

speaker: interviewee
text: I was so young that it's very hard to imagine. Had I been further along in school, you know, in a high school situation maybe. I don't know. What I have been? What would I have done with my life? So you know, I don't know. But I feel that I have grown into the best person that I could be. And of course, I have now been married for 46 years, so that, that, too, has been a fabulous experience.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes we'll meet your husband at the end of this tape in a moment, and do the photographs. Before we break for that, is there anything else you'd like to talk about that I didn't ask? Any other images or ideas?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, it's just that, although my story is not one of utter horror, or you know what was gone through, it's important to know that this was all part of it-- families being split up, and families being torn apart. Many of them not being able to be united. My fortune is that my mother and my sister at least survived. Of course, there were no cousins, no aunts, no uncles. And it was a void. It was a, it was a-- there was a big void in my life because of that. And now in the later years, you know, you hear people talking of cousins, of family circles. I don't have one. So it's a, you know, it's a very different concept and-- My children were fortunate enough to have at least a grandmother. And that's on both sides because my husband also is from Europe. But they never knew what it was to have a grandfather. And I did. I walked with my grandfather to his clinic to have his urine tested, or blood tested, for his diabetes. And I have that pleasure. They did not.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well we'll your husband then in a moment. Thank you, Mrs. Rosenstock.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. Sitting next to me now is Walter Rosenstock, my husband of 46 years. Walter came to this country in 1936, which will make it 60 years this year, Walt.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right. Fortunately, we were able to escape the horror of the Nazi regime in 1936. Which our relatives were able to sponsor us to come to the States.

speaker: interviewee
text: Walter had a great aunt and uncle who sponsored not only his family of five people, but other families. And they were able to escape before, although-- excuse me-- my father-in-law was beaten up. Right, dear?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, mm hm. That's what made us decide to get-- to leave Germany at that particular time. The town we lived in was a small village, Miltenberg am Main. From-- everybody was family, and knew one another. But the fascists came in from out of town and took care of the Jews. And their method, which made the decision quite simple to leave.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any message that you'd want your children and grandchildren to pick up from this? Need some water? Yeah, question, is there anything you'd want to communicate to you children and grandchildren about what you and your wife went through?

speaker: interviewee
text: Our children should definitely remember what has happened to their parents, and what they went through specifically, so it will not reoccur in the future, and do all they can to prevent it. To see that the hatred is something that we can manage and possibly eliminate.

speaker: interviewee
text: They're living in the greatest country. They can vote. They can vote and make their voices heard when things are wrong. And they have the ability to right them. That's one of the important things that was missed. People were taken in, were gullible. And I think, with our communication today, we, we have it in our hands to see to it that these kind of things will never happen again anywhere in the world and must not happen anywhere in the world. Each one of us is responsible. .

speaker: interviewee
text: We have to participate in licking this. We have to speak out, which probably was not the case many years ago. People did not take part and did not speak out, which is very important.

speaker: interviewee
text: There'll always be man's inhumanity to man. There is always going to be someone in the pecking order who wants to be on top and wants to destroy. And we can't let it happen. That's, I think, the message for any of us who have gone through these periods.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, let's look at the photographs now.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: Those are my grandparents on my mother's side, Alma and Saloman Hirsch from Bad Wildungen,

speaker: interviewer
text: OK

speaker: interviewee
text: Albert Herz, my father. That picture was taken as an identification picture. And in those days, the ID pictures were all with the left ear showing. This is my mother, Flora Herz. Again, it's an ID photograph. And her left ear is showing. Left to right, Edith after she came back from concentration camp. In the middle, Edith as I knew her when I left Germany. Again, that's was her identification picture with the left ear showing. And the right hand corner is me-- one of the pictures from England.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: The three people standing, from left to right, are my grandfather, my Aunt Alice, who emigrated to America in 1937, and my mother. And the little girl is me. And it was probably around 1936 or '37. I was five or six years old, in front of my grandparents' house in Bad Wildungen. This picture was taken in Rhyl, North Wales, R-H-Y-L. It's called a walking picture. And from left to right is my foster brother David at 13, 14 months old; my foster father George Parry; Florence Perry, my foster mother; and Suse. Because we were stateless, there was no such thing as a passport. This is my certificate of identity, issued in Britain before I came to the United States. And this was in lieu of a passport upon my entry to the United States in 1946. This is the most important document-- outside of my marriage license-- that I shall ever own. It is the telegram that told me that my mother and my sister had come back from concentration camp alive-- barely, but alive. And the envelope is in my sister's handwriting. She had remembered my address in her head through all the horrors that she went through. This is the document that I think will be one of the most important documents I shall ever own besides my marriage license. It is the telegram that told me that my mother and my sister had survived concentration camp. The address on the envelope is written in my sister's handwriting. She had remembered my address through all the years of horror that she had experienced. And it was, as they say, a sight for sore eyes when I saw it.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the telegram in 1944 that gave me hope that part of my family was still alive. From this, I said that my mother and my sister are alive, and my father is dead. And indeed, my ESP was correct.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Standing from left to right are Walter, and Deborah, Alan Peizer holding Larissa, his wife-- my daughter-- Elaine, our son Alan, Donna-- Alan's wife-- holding Max. And seated in front is Suse and Ross Peizer, Elaine and Alan's son.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: These are son Alan's two boys. On the left is Max, and on the right is Sam. They are two years apart.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: These are daughter Elaine's two children. On the left is Larissa, and on the right is Ross Peizer. And they are five years apart.
